chapters.title: Chips are vital but vulnerable
chapters.text:
It's incredible how technology the same size as a rice grain can tremendously transform our lives. Smartphones, GPS, and MRI scanners are only some of the countless benefits of chips, also known as integrated circuits. They account for American leadership in the military sphere and the Asian economic boom. Their “fathers” founded Silicon Valley. But despite their vast influence on our lives, hardly any economic field depends so immensely on just a few companies.

Silicon Valley received its name from the material, which, together with billions of transistors, constitutes the core of integrated circuits.

For example, logic chips process the data, being the “brains” of devices. In 2019, Taiwan manufactured 37% of them. As for memory chips, the world relies heavily on two Korean companies, which produce almost half of these integrated circuits. Moreover, top-notch technology is impossible without extreme ultraviolet lithography machines. All of them come from the Dutch company ASML.

In the age of AI, it's often said that data is the new oil. Yet the real limitation we face isn't the availability of data but of processing power. ~ Chris Miller, Prof.

This distribution of resources provides mentioned countries with enormous profits but simultaneously poses serious risks, concentrating critical manufacturing in one area. If something goes wrong, like in the case of a powerful earthquake, it will bring advanced chip production to a halt. Moreover, people can prepare facilities for natural disasters, but what about human-made ones? The 2022 Chinese military exercises around Taiwan are warning bells for its citizens and the rest of the world.

How has this critical sphere become so vulnerable? How did China, which during Mao's radicalism made scientists work as farmers, become a game changer in high tech? What tools exist to maintain the balance of power? Join our investigation to discover the answers to these and many more questions.

chapters.title: The invention of chips is half the battle; finding a market for them is the other
chapters.text:
The story of integrated circuits started in the middle of the 20th century. As the need for complex calculations grew, scientists devoted themselves to finding faster ways of crunching numbers. Specifically, they focused on improving computers, which were cumbersome then.

Semiconductors were the solution to the problem, but they puzzled engineers' minds. These materials, like silicon and germanium, have dual nature. In their normal state, they have low conductivity. Yet, if you add phosphorus to semiconductors and use an electric field, they start to conduct electricity. Moreover, in 1948, William Shockley, an American physicist, discovered how to control the issue. He created a device with three semiconductor layers; by manipulating the middle one, he could switch this electric current off and on. It was the birth of transistors, which started to replace vacuum tubes.

In the late 1950s, two engineers took devices to the next level by creating integrated circuits. Instead of using separate pieces for each transistor, they built a few transistors into one piece of germanium or silicon. The first inventor was Jack Kilby, who worked at Texas Instruments. Robert Noyce from Fairchild Semiconductor was the second. He founded this company with seven colleagues after they resigned from Shockley's lab. These “traitorous eight” are often called the founders of Silicon Valley.

The only problem with integrated circuits was the price. Companies desperately needed wealthy customers, and Soviet Sputnik, the first artificial Earth satellite, helped to find them. Its launch alarmed the American government, perpetuating the fear of falling behind. So, officials turned to Texas Instruments and Fairchild for cutting-edge chips. Consequently, integrated circuits became crucial for many military projects, from guiding missiles to taking Apollo 11 to the moon. However, engineers dreamed of the mass market.

Reaching this aim became possible when electronics scientist, Jay Lathrop, patented photolithography. This method of using light to create small and detailed patterns in a material decreased the size of chips. So, when the government reduced funding, companies switched to the mass market, where computer sales rocketed.

Fairchild's first integrated circuit for the private sector was Zenith's hearing aid chip, which they initially devised for a NASA satellite.

chapters.title: The cost of cheap labor in technology is tricky
chapters.text:
Discoveries of transistors and photolithography guaranteed the US's leadership in the chip industry. Its top-notch integrated circuits and the best computers made other countries lag behind. Confident in their future dominance, American companies started to transfer technology abroad.

Why? The first reason was economic. The US needed to decrease the cost of chips to obtain more customers. A cheaper workforce was a viable solution. As a result, Fairchild offshored assembling to Hong Kong, where salaries were ten times lower. Others quickly mirrored this trend. Soon companies also negotiated with Singapore, Korea, Malaysia, and Taiwan to assemble chips there.

It seemed like a win-win deal. On the one hand, Asian countries reduced unemployment. For instance, electronics constituted almost 10% of Singapore's GNP and 25% of all its manufacturing jobs. Additionally, the presence of American businesses in these countries increased the chances that the US would protect them. At the same time, lower prices enabled the thriving of Fairchild and other firms.

Another reason for cooperation with Asian countries was geopolitical. Americans wanted to ensure the Soviet Union wouldn't obtain new allies during the Cold War. It was so crucial that the US even refused to ban Japan's high tech to punish the country for its role in World War II. This policy assisted Sony, the Japanese company, in getting the license to produce the transistors.

Interconnections between countries' economies are an effective leverage for geopolitical influence.

The US didn't see Japan as a serious competitor, believing it couldn't make better chips than Americans. Indeed, the Land of the Rising Sun didn't initially excel in integrated circuits, but it became a leader in designing products that contain them. For instance, Sony launched extremely successful transistor radios and calculators.

At the end of the 1970s, American dominance started to fade. More Japanese researchers received Nobel prizes for innovations, and the country's chip quality was better. On top of that, Sony's Walkman, a portable music player, became one of the most popular devices in history. Japan's success stemmed from a couple of factors:

• Cheap credit for companies
• Government support
• Protected national market: In Japan, quotas imposed limits on American products

As a result, although Americans invented memory chips (DRAM), their market share was 1.7% in the 1980s. This status quo remained until one pivotal alarm.

chapters.title: Steps that helped the US surpass Japan
chapters.text:
In 1989, a Japanese collection of essays made the US “wake up.” Texts written by a far-right politician, Shintaro Ishihara, toyed with the idea of using chips to weaken the American influence and become a leader instead. It wasn't limited to economics. The author also suggested abandoning US restrictions and building a powerful army. Moreover, similar ideas in less radical forms appeared in conversations with other, less controversial Japanese politicians. Americans immediately took measures to redress the balance.

Firstly, they concentrated on innovation. The following are just a few examples:

• Scientists Lynn Conway and Carver Mead devised a set of rules contributing to integrated circuit design automation.
• A new American company, Micron, dared to challenge Japanese dominance in the DRAM field by cutting costs. Reducing the size of the chip gave them a huge advantage. Additionally, they simplified manufacturing steps to reduce errors and save time.

Nowadays, the world relies on three U.S.-based companies — Cadence, Synopsys, and Mentor — specializing in chip design software.

Secondly, manufacturers changed their business models. Intel, founded by Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore, invented DRAMs but couldn't keep up with the Japanese in manufacturing them. Therefore, Intel abandoned these integrated circuits and focused on microprocessors. After signing the contract with IBM, Intel became a world leader, establishing a monopoly in x86 chips for personal computers (PCs).

Another crucial step for American companies was moving more facilities to Korea and Taiwan, where the workforce was cheap. This way, the USA wanted to weaken the Japanese market share. Korea's firm Samsung made the most of this American-Japanese rivalry. For instance, they bought a license for designing cutting-edge DRAM from Micron.

These steps helped the US to improve its position in the market. At the same time, its success also stemmed from the failures of Japanese firms. With huge sales, they neglected innovation. For example, thanks to Intel, we know about the “flash” or NAND chip, which stores data even when the power is off. Yet Intel wasn't the first to create it. The actual innovator is Fujio Masuoka, but the company he worked for, Toshiba, underestimated this discovery. At the same time, Japan's most severe mistake was ignoring PCs.

Overall, the US became a leader in semiconductor shipments in 1993. Korea took second place five years later.

chapters.title: While Taiwanese and Dutch firms experimented, Intel became a hostage of its monopoly
chapters.text:
The dominance in the chip industry rests significantly on innovation. Using “extreme ultraviolet” (EUV) light to make integrated circuits smaller would be a huge advantage. But it was an unattainable aim for American lithography toolmakers. GCA, a top company at the beginning of the 1980s, had lost its leadership. The main reason was poor management, not rivalry with Japanese firms like Canon and Nikon.

This state of affairs gave the Dutch company ASML a chance to get its market share. Three factors contributed to its success:

• Excellent supply chain management: Many companies worldwide shipped the best components to the Netherlands.
• Outstanding skills: A EUV machine, introduced by ASML, comprised 457,329 parts.
• A neutral location: It ensured immense investment from the US, in particular Intel. The latter transferred four billion dollars to ASML's account in 2012.

The Dutch firm also received financing from another client that could afford top-notch technology — Samsung. With a license for modern DRAM, officials' support, and cheap credits, Korea became one of the central powers in the integrated circuits industry. Yet soon, Intel and Samsung had to share their leadership with a company founded in Taiwan.

Realizing that the opening of Chinese economics and its low wages would encourage American firms to assemble their chips there, Taiwanese officials acted decisively. Minister K. T. Li invited Morris Chang, a former senior executive at Texas Instruments, to found and run the company TSMC. The government gave almost half the money for the startup and “asked” Taiwanese millionaires to provide the rest. Soon they gathered the needed amount.

It was a golden opportunity for Chang to embody his ultimate dream — a company focusing only on manufacturing chips that other companies designed. He knew that the burden of equipment cost prevented many entrepreneurs from launching their businesses. TSMC would solve their problems, becoming their faithful ally. This model turned out to be tremendously successful.

Meanwhile, Intel's role weakened. Relying heavily on the x86 chips monopoly, it ignored new spheres, like mobile phones and artificial intelligence. Moreover, combining manufacturing and designing was challenging, which influenced the quality of products. Consequently, Intel didn't make the most of EUV, despite its massive investments in it. American dominance was at risk again, especially when China joined the race for leadership in integrated circuits.

Intel could have made chips for iPhones but declined Steve Jobs's offer, considering it unprofitable.

chapters.title: Chinese high-tech success takes root in Western support
chapters.text:
Under Mao Zedong, the People's Republic of China lagged far behind the world chip industry. The government didn't invest in science and banned everything foreign. As a result, in 1975, only one in a thousand of its integrated circuits worked well. After opening its economy, China relied heavily on other countries in the chip sphere. But soon it found a way to change this.

U.S. and UK chip architectures and designs as well as Taiwanese foundries have played a central role in the development of China's supercomputer programs. ~ Chris Miller, Prof.

China turned its dependence on foreign integrated circuits into an advantage by using its market as leverage for influence. For instance, the latter became a decisive factor in establishing a joint venture between the American company Qualcomm and its Chinese counterpart Huaxintong. Sometimes the price for market access was trading of technology — that's what IBM did in 2015. This way, it strived to improve its sales since they dropped by 20% in China. Moreover, IBM sold almost all its stakes in its facilities in China and Malaysia to Chinese firms.

These policies contributed to the rise of Huawei. Its success also stemmed from other factors:

• Immense governmental support
• Strategy — building commodities with better quality and at a lower price
• Western experts' recommendations
• Tremendous R&D budget
• Leadership in providing equipment for cell towers

Although Huawei earned less than a billion dollars in 1999, its expenditure on consultants was 50 million.

Nevertheless, Huawei's reputation was dubious. For example, experts accused the company of spying on behalf of the Chinese government. These close ties between private firms and the state became a severe concern to American officials.

Another warning bell was the story of the Chinese company Jinhua, which stole confidential Micron files and patented these inventions in China. When the US sued Jinhua, it did the same in a local court, accusing Micron of violating “their intellectual property.” Chinese judges found the American company guilty. Moreover, they prohibited Micron from selling 26 commodities in their country. The US responded immediately. It banned Jinhua from buying American equipment for manufacturing, which led to the company's decline.

Did you know? According to think-tank MacroPolo, almost a third of the best AI experts are Chinese.

chapters.title: All roads in the chip industry lead to Taiwan
chapters.text:
Although American companies became dependent on the Chinese market, they hardly mentioned it in conversations with the government. Instead, they tried to normalize the state of affairs and save their profits. Yet the fact that their fiercest competitor provided their biggest market posed many risks, not only economical. Chinese firms rested on state financing, which made them tools to reach geopolitical goals.

Experts worldwide started to acknowledge this. For instance, in Australia, they recommended banning Huawei's 5G networks as the risks to national security were high. At first, the country's prime minister considered it a radical solution, but he soon changed his mind. Other countries, like Japan and France, followed this trend.

Their concerns were predictable — Huawei became a giant of high-tech. In 30 years, it caught up with leaders and provided advanced telecom and networking gear. Moreover, it designed top-notch smartphone chips and spent significant sums on development, similar to Google's R&D. Huawei also felt confident in foreign markets. At the same time, it faced numerous accusations of bribing executives to win profitable contracts.

Considering the connections with China's state, the company's success was threatening. Discovering that Huawei violated American sanctions on Iran was a turning point. The US decided to use its influence, stemming from:

• Monopoly in chip software
• Irreplaceable light sources for ASML
• Advanced fabrication plants in countries that depend on US protection (Samsung and TSMC)

As a result, in 2020, Huawei couldn't buy any commodities made with the help of American technology. All advanced chips were beyond its reach. Indeed, these constraints undermined its success significantly.

Apple and Huawei have been the main customers of TSMC for years. Yet the 2022 Chinese military exercises around Taiwan remind us how fragile the power balance is.

On the other hand, American restrictions encouraged China to invest even more in high-tech; China supported research on RISC-V, a new architecture to weaken the American monopoly on x86 chips. Chinese companies have a good chance of a share in the NAND market and modern logic chips. Yet they still depend on the US and, most importantly, Taiwan.

The latter also plays a crucial role for American firms, especially Intel. TSMC is vital for its manufacturing of cutting-edge chips. Producing them by themselves is too expensive for China and the USA.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
In 1965, Gordon Moore said that the number of transistors in a chip would double every two years, making prices lower. He also envisioned that high-tech devices would be widespread, which sounded unthinkable then. Now we see how correct he was — technology is integral to our lifestyles. Although fabrication plants produced 13% more integrated circuits in 2021 than in 2020, the demand was enormous due to working online. Thus, the shortage of chips during the COVID-19 pandemic affected many spheres.

Despite its crucial role, the chip industry rests only on a few key players. Their interconnections present risks but simultaneously constitute leverage to maintain the balance. The latter is critical: a shift may change the global economy.

Companies know that harnessing innovations is the only way to save or improve their positions in the market. So, we will soon witness even more breakthroughs. What exactly can we expect in the near future?

• Cutting-edge 3D transistor structures may lose ground to new tube-shaped ones.
• Microprocessor engineer Jim Keller, famous for his work at AMD and Apple, has found a way to increase the density of transistors in chips by 50 times.
• Some scientists believe that customized chips will surpass general-purpose ones. Many big tech companies have already devised integrated circuits.
• Advances in technology will make artificial intelligence easier and cheaper to use.

How will all these breakthroughs change our society? Soon, we'll find out. We live in an exciting time when researchers may outpace science fiction writers.

Try this


• Choose media about high-tech and read it weekly to catch up with the latest updates.
• Do some research to discover what types of chips your device uses, who designed it, and where it was manufactured.
• Watch a TedTalk about chips of the future.
